question.text: In which derivative market is the concept of 'cheapest-to-deliver' (CTD) bond relevant?

answer.text: Interest rate futures — where the seller can choose which eligible bond to deliver, selecting the one that minimizes cost
Equity derivatives — where the seller delivers the cheapest stock from the index basket
Currency derivatives — where the seller delivers the cheapest currency pair
Commodity derivatives — where the seller delivers the lowest-grade commodity

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Bond futures contracts allow delivery of any bond from a basket of eligible deliverable bonds. The seller chooses the bond with the lowest cost of delivery, known as the cheapest-to-deliver (CTD) bond. The conversion factor adjusts the settlement price for different coupons and maturities. **The Trap:** CTD is unique to bond futures — equity, currency, and commodity derivatives are cash-settled or have fixed delivery specifications. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series IV Ch3 covers the CTD concept extensively. Series XIII tests whether students understand which market-specific concepts apply to which derivative type. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The CTD bond changes as yields move — when yields rise, longer duration bonds tend to become CTD; when yields fall, shorter duration bonds become CTD.
